description:
A colleague on the API-integration team has asked you to ship a “snapshot” of all current
    raw log files so that they can replay traffic in their staging environment.
    Everything you need is already in place under your home directory.

    1. Source data  
       • Directory:  /home/user/api_logs  
       • Files inside that directory (all are plain UTF-8 text):  
         a) request.log  
         b) response.log  
         c) errors.log  

    2. Your deliverables  
       You must produce exactly TWO new artefacts in /home/user (do **NOT** move or modify
       the original log files):  

       a) A gzip-compressed tar archive named:  
          /home/user/api_logs_backup.tar.gz  

          • The archive must sit directly in /home/user (not in a sub-directory).  
          • The archive root must be the **three files only** – there must be no leading
            “api_logs/” directory path preserved inside the archive; in other words, when the
            archive is listed with “tar -tzf”, it should output exactly:  
                errors.log  
                request.log  
                response.log  
            (each on its own line, in that alphabetical order).  
          • The archive must contain byte-for-byte copies of the source files.

       b) A plain-text verification file named:  
          /home/user/archive_contents.txt  

          • Format: one file name per line, sorted in case-sensitive alphabetical order, with
            no extra whitespace. The file must therefore look **exactly** like this (three lines, trailing
            newline after the last line is optional):  
                errors.log  
                request.log  
                response.log  

    3. Constraints  
       • Accomplish the compression in a **single** terminal command (including any necessary
         options or subshells).  
       • You do NOT need super-user privileges.  
       • Do not delete, rename, or relocate the original /home/user/api_logs directory.  
       • After creating the archive, generate the verification file using any commands you like.

    4. What the automated checker will validate  
       • /home/user/api_logs_backup.tar.gz exists and is a valid gzip-compressed tar archive.  
       • The archive’s internal file list matches exactly the three file names above, with no
         directory prefixes and in the specified alphabetical order.  
       • /home/user/archive_contents.txt exists and contains the exact three-line listing
         specified above.  

    When you are finished, you can use the usual shell commands (e.g., “tar -tzf …”, “cat …”)
    to demonstrate that the outputs meet all requirements, but that is optional.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing state -->
    <initial>
        <directory path="/home/user/api_logs"/>
        <file path="/home/user/api_logs/request.log" content="GET /v1/data 200 OK\n"/>
        <file path="/home/user/api_logs/response.log" content="HTTP/1.1 200 OK\nContent-Type: application/json\n{ \"status\": \"ok\" }\n"/>
        <file path="/home/user/api_logs/errors.log" content="[Error] 504 Gateway Timeout at 2023-02-01T12:34:56Z\n"/>
    </initial>

    <!-- Expected artefacts after agent work -->
    <expected>
        <file path="/home/user/api_logs_backup.tar.gz" must_exist="true"/>
        <file path="/home/user/archive_contents.txt" exact_content="errors.log\nrequest.log\nresponse.log\n"/>
    </expected>
name: task_000000_fb005d00
